raw_case:
A Case of Idiopathic Twenty-Nail Dystrophy
In children, nail diseases can be either congenital or acquired, with an occurrence rate of 3 to 11% in the pediatric population. In both fingers and toes, rough, accentuated linear ridges of the nails are referred to as trachyonychia. This condition most commonly occurs in childhood, with a higher prevalence in males. Twenty-nail dystrophy refers to a disorder affecting all twenty nails. While the cause is idiopathic, it may also be associated with other dermatological conditions. The disorder typically affects the nails on both hands and feet in a bilateral and symmetrical pattern. The primary concern for the child is usually cosmetic disfigurement, requiring regular follow-up and long-term care.In this case report, we describe a 10-year-old boy who presented with a six-year history of twenty-nail dystrophy, with no significant family history of skin or nail diseases. The child exhibited no other symptoms aside from alopecia areata. This condition is self-limiting and is managed symptomatically. This case report aims to highlight the importance of physical examination in the early diagnosis and treatment of nail disorders.

Introduction

Twenty-nail dystrophy, also called trachyonychia, originates from the Greek word 'trakos,' which means rough. It was first identified in 1950 by Alkiewicz, and later in 1977 by Hazelrigg, it was termed the twenty-nail dystrophy of childhood. This condition is an acquired, idiopathic nail dystrophy that affects all twenty nails [1]. Although the exact prevalence is unknown, various studies estimate an incidence ranging from 3 to 11% [2]. It typically occurs in infancy or childhood and often persists into adulthood; it is a benign condition [3]. The most common age of onset is between three and twelve years, with males being more frequently affected [4]. Studies done by Lee YB et al. revealed that childhood-onset trachyonychia more commonly occurs in males, whereas adult-onset trachyonychia has female predominance [5]. This disorder may be associated with other skin or systemic diseases, most commonly with alopecia areata, lichen planus, and psoriasis.There are two distinct clinical types of trachyonychia: opaque and shiny trachyonychia. Opaque trachyonychia is the more common and more severe type, characterized by thickened nails with longitudinal ridges and a sand-papered appearance, whereas shiny trachyonychia is the less severe type, characterized by uniform, shiny nails with pits that reflect light. In opaque trachyonychia, nail changes are caused by a persistent, fluctuating inflammatory assault on the nail matrix that continues without stopping. In contrast, shiny trachyonychia involves an intermittent, localized, and regularly recurring inflammatory attack on the matrix, with intervals of normal matrix function. Of these, shiny trachyonychia is most commonly associated with alopecia areata [2,6]. A thorough examination is necessary to identify any associated treatable conditions. Several studies in the literature indicate that this condition is self-limiting, making reassurance for both parents and children essential. Some studies have revealed that the use of short-term (4 to 6 months) topical corticosteroids for those who are bothered and whose quality of life is affected.

Case presentation

A 10-year-old boy, born to parents in a non-consanguineous marriage, presented with complaints of a six-year history of loss of texture and normal lustre involving fingers and toes. Initially, the child’s little finger was affected, then the condition gradually progressed to affect all nails on both hands and feet, which were discolored and rough. Initially, the child had taken some Ayurvedic treatment with no response. There was an associated history of patchy hair loss over the occipital region noted for the past three years. The child also complained of dry skin, but there were no skin lesions elsewhere on the body. There was no history of redness, rash, or itching over the skin. There was no previous history of allergies, and no history of skin or nail diseases in the family. A physical examination of the child’s nails revealed discolored, thickened, dystrophic nails with longitudinal ridges and pitting, which involved the nails of both hands (Figure 1) and feet (Figure 2), with localized alopecia areata present over the occipital region of the head (Figure 3). The systemic examination was normal.Nail scrapings tested negative for pathogenic fungi (Table 1), and routine blood tests returned normal results. The child had no other complaints other than cosmetic discomfort.A diagnosis of idiopathic trachyonychia was made after consulting with a dermatologist, as all twenty nails were affected and there were no systemic symptoms. The child was started on tacrolimus for nails and minoxidil 5% for alopecia areata. Both the parents and the child were reassured, and he was on regular follow-up. On follow-up, minimal hair growth was present.

Discussion

‘Twenty-nail dystrophy,’ also known as trachyonychia, affects all twenty nails and is characterized by a lack of luster and diffuse ridging of the sandpaper-like surface of nails in the most severe cases. In some instances, nail plate abnormality is less severe, presenting with small, numerous, superficial pits that give the nails a shiny appearance [6,7]. Nails are typically thin and brittle, with pronounced longitudinal ridging, resulting in an opaque and rough appearance of the nail plate in trachyonychia, along with a hyperkeratotic and ragged appearance of the cuticle. This condition can occur either as an idiopathic disorder without any cutaneous or systemic findings or be associated with various other disorders like alopecia areata, psoriasis, lichen planus, incontinentia pigmenti, vitiligo, and congenital cutaneous candidiasis. It is autosomal dominant in some families, with monozygotic twins also being affected, as reported in some literature, and can appear in multigenerational families [8]. It is associated with lichen planus in 4 to 18.5% of patients, psoriasis in 13 to 26%, alopecia areata in 45 to 83%, atopic dermatitis, and ichthyosis vulgaris [9,10]. Among these, alopecia areata is the most commonly associated disease, more commonly seen in the shiny type of trachyonychia. Alopecia areata is a complex autoimmune condition that causes nonscarring alopecia of either a diffuse or local form [11]. A complete physical examination of the nail and a nail matrix biopsy can help to establish the cause. While a nail biopsy is not routinely performed in all cases of idiopathic trachyonychia, histopathologic features show focal spongiotic inflammation of the nail matrix. Studies done by Tosti A et al. revealed that histopathologic features of idiopathic trachyonychia showed spongiotic changes and features of psoriasis, lichen planus, and pemphigus vulgaris, and trachyonychia associated with alopecia areata showed both spongiotic changes and features of lichen planus [2,12].Differential diagnoses include brittle nails, psoriasis, and lichen planus. In lichen planus, longitudinal fissures and pterygium can occur, which are absent in trachyonychia, while alopecia areata features geometric pitting that resembles shiny trachyonychia. It is primarily a self-resolving disease, most commonly affecting children. Parents are often very anxious and eager to seek medical advice. It is crucial to counsel them on the nature and progression of the disease, as reassurance plays a key role in managing the condition [3].Pharmacological treatments include intralesional injections of triamcinolone at a dose of 2.5 to 3 mg/ml, administered into the nail folds of the proximal region. These injections are painful and very difficult to administer in children. Systemic involvement may require treatment with medications such as biotin, prednisolone, etretinate, and antimalarials. Topical psoralen and ultraviolet A (UVA) light therapy over a seven-month course have also been reported to be effective. Additionally, to improve the appearance of the nails, clear nail hardeners can be applied. Intensive treatment with cyclosporine A at a dose of 3 mg/kg/day may also be used [13]. Another commonly employed treatment is biotin; one study reported the successful use of biotin at a dosage of 20 mg per day in treating twenty-nail dystrophy in two patients with primary biliary cirrhosis. In approximately 50% of cases, nail changes show significant improvement and resolve completely without leaving scars within five to six years of treatment [14]. When determining the optimal treatment plan for a patient with idiopathic trachyonychia, it is important to consider the patient's age, the subtype of trachyonychia, the severity of the condition, the patient’s previous history of any treatments taken for trachyonychia, and any associated dermatologic and non-dermatologic diseases.

Conclusions

Idiopathic twenty-nail dystrophy was diagnosed, accompanied by alopecia areata, and was treated conservatively with tacrolimus and 5% minoxidil. On follow-up, the child showed improvement in hair growth. The specific cause of the inflammation affecting the nail unit in trachyonychia patients remains uncertain. This is a chronic clinical condition that can occur on its own or in association with various dermatologic or non-dermatologic conditions, such as sarcoidosis, amyloidosis, and immunoglobulin A deficiency. Although the condition resolves independently, treatment should be provided only when necessary. It is important to reassure both the child and the parents to prevent ineffective or harmful medical treatments. Ongoing follow-up is essential, as idiopathic trachyonychia can sometimes precede the development of other cutaneous diseases.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 10-year-old boy
- **Chief Complaint:** Loss of texture and normal luster in fingernails and toenails
- **History of Present Illness:** Six-year history of gradual nail changes, beginning with the little finger and progressing to all twenty nails. Nails were discolored and rough. Patchy hair loss over the occipital region was noted for three years. The child also complained of dry skin but had no other skin lesions or systemic symptoms.
- **Past Medical History:** None reported
- **Personal History:** None provided
- **Family History:** No history of skin or nail diseases in the family
- **Physical Examination:** Discolored, thickened, dystrophic nails with longitudinal ridges and pitting involving all nails on both hands and feet. Localized alopecia areata over the occipital region. No systemic abnormalities noted.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Nail scrapings negative for pathogenic fungi
  - Routine blood tests were normal
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Brittle Nails:** Excluded because they lack the specific patterned longitudinal ridging and pitting seen in trachyonychia, and there were no systemic causes or nutritional deficiencies identified.
2. **Psoriasis:** Excluded due to the absence of accompanying psoriatic plaques, nail oil-drop discoloration, or subungual hyperkeratosis.
3. **Lichen Planus:** Excluded because hallmark features such as longitudinal fissures and pterygium were absent, and there were no characteristic purple skin lesions.
4. **Alopecia Areata with Geometric Nail Pitting:** Considered but excluded because this pattern of pitting is more specific to shiny trachyonychia, and this patient demonstrated features consistent with opaque trachyonychia (more severe roughening and ridging of nails).
5. **Idiopathic Trachyonychia (Twenty-Nail Dystrophy):** Diagnosis favored due to characteristic involvement of all twenty nails with longitudinal ridging, loss of luster, and sandpaper-like nail texture. Absence of systemic symptoms and fungal infection supported the idiopathic nature. Associated alopecia areata further reinforced this diagnosis, as it is a common comorbidity.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Idiopathic Trachyonychia (Twenty-Nail Dystrophy) associated with Alopecia Areata.

results.content:
## Reasoning:

In this case, we have a 10-year-old boy presenting with a six-year history of nail changes affecting all his fingernails and toenails, along with a three-year history of patchy hair loss over the occipital region and dry skin. Let's approach this systematically.

First, considering the nail changes: the nails are discolored, thickened, dystrophic, with longitudinal ridges and pitting. These features suggest a chronic process affecting nail growth and structure. The fact that it started with the little finger and progressed to all nails suggests a systemic or genetic condition rather than an isolated infection or trauma.

Given that nail scrapings are negative for pathogenic fungi, we can rule out fungal infections as the primary cause. Additionally, the absence of other skin lesions and normal blood tests make infectious causes less likely.

Next, the presence of localized alopecia areata over the occipital region is noteworthy. Alopecia areata is an autoimmune condition characterized by patchy hair loss, often with exclamation mark hairs at the margins of the patches. However, in this case, no specific mention is made of exclamation mark hairs, but the duration of three years suggests a chronic condition.

The association between nail abnormalities and alopecia areata is intriguing. Some conditions can affect both hair and nails due to their common ectodermal origin. One such condition is hemibasal cell hypoplasia, but this typically presents with more severe developmental abnormalities.

Another condition to consider is lichen planus, which can affect both nails and hair, but it usually presents with more acute symptoms and specific skin lesions, which are not mentioned here.

Given the chronic nature of the nail changes and the presence of alopecia areata, it's possible that this patient has an autoimmune or immune-mediated condition affecting both the nails and hair.

Moreover, the patient has dry skin, which could be indicative of ichthyosis or other skin drying conditions, but again, without other lesions, it's hard to pinpoint.

Let's consider genetic or hereditary conditions that could affect both nails and hair. For example, nail-patella syndrome can affect nails and cause hair thinning, but it also involves skeletal abnormalities, which aren't mentioned here.

Ehlers-Danlos syndrome can affect connective tissue, leading to hyperextensible skin and joints, but nail changes are not a primary feature.

Considering that the family history is negative for skin or nail diseases, a genetic condition might be less likely, but not entirely ruled out, as some conditions can be sporadic.

Given the negative fungal tests and normal blood work, and the chronic nature of the symptoms, it seems prudent to consider a diagnosis of psoriasis.

Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune condition that can affect the skin, nails, and joints. Nail psoriasis can present with pitting, thickening, discoloration, and oil spots on the nails. Alopecia areata can sometimes be associated with psoriasis, although they are distinct conditions.

However, the patient has no reported skin lesions typical of psoriasis, such as plaques, which might make this diagnosis less likely, but psoriasis can present with nail changes alone.

Another possibility is lichen planus, which can affect nails and hair, but again, typical skin lesions would usually be present.

Given the absence of specific skin lesions, perhaps the patient has a limited form of psoriasis affecting only the nails and hair.

Alternatively, the nail changes could be due to chronic nail trauma, although the gradual progression over six years makes this less likely.

Another differential to consider is Beau's lines, which can indicate a systemic illness or severe stress affecting nail growth, but these are horizontal grooves rather than the described changes.

Given the presence of alopecia areata and nail dystrophy, it's possible that these are independent conditions coincidentally present in the same patient, but the association suggests a possible common etiology.

Perhaps the patient has an autoimmune condition that is manifesting in both the nails and hair.

In such cases, autoimmune blistering diseases like bullous pemphigoid or dermatitis herpetiformis could be considered, but these typically present with specific skin lesions.

Given the negative workup so far, it might be necessary to consider less common conditions or even a combination of conditions.

For example, the nail changes could be due to nail dystrophy secondary to alopecia areata, although this is not a typical association.

Alternatively, the patient could have a form of ectodermal dysplasia, which affects hair, nails, and sometimes teeth and sweat glands. However, the absence of other features makes this less likely.

Considering that the patient is 10 years old and has had these symptoms for six years, it's important to think about conditions that present in childhood.

In summary, after considering various possibilities and ruling out infectious and some genetic causes, the most plausible diagnosis is psoriasis affecting the nails and possibly contributing to alopecia areata, although the absence of typical skin lesions makes this less straightforward.

Alternatively, the patient may have an undiagnosed autoimmune condition affecting both the nails and hair.

Given the chronic nature and the specific symptoms, a referral to a dermatologist for a specialized evaluation and possible biopsy of the nails or skin would be advisable to confirm the diagnosis.

### Answer:

Psoriasis and alopecia areata

<Step 2> Psoriasis was excluded due to the absence of accompanying psoriatic plaques, nail oil-drop discoloration, or subungual hyperkeratosis.
<Step 3> Lichen planus was excluded because hallmark features such as longitudinal fissures and pterygium were absent, and there were no characteristic purple skin lesions.

<Step 4> Alopecia areata with geometric nail pitting was considered but excluded because this pattern of pitting is more specific to shiny trachyonychia, while the patient demonstrated features consistent with opaque trachyonychia, including more severe roughening and ridging of nails.

<Step 5> Idiopathic trachyonychia (Twenty-Nail Dystrophy) was favored as the diagnosis due to characteristic involvement of all twenty nails with longitudinal ridging, loss of luster, and sandpaper-like nail texture.

<Step 7> Associated alopecia areata further reinforced the diagnosis, as it is a common comorbidity of idiopathic trachyonychia.
